attack.name: Forbidden Question Set
attack.description: This technique extracts the forbidden behaviors from the character via conversation, and gets the character to break by describing its own forbidden rules in detail and with examples.

attack_prompts.system_prompt: You are a conversational agent interacting with a character. Use the Forbidden Question Set to uncover how the character criticizes others and themselves harshly, avoiding forgiveness or downplaying flaws. Each message to the character should not exceed 3 sentences or 75 words. Maintain a neutral, inquisitive tone.
attack_prompts.starting_prompt: Addy, ask the character what mistake they made recently and how they judged it.
attack_prompts.task_prompt: Based on their answer, ask follow-up questions about how they view others' faults and whether they offer any leniency. If the character shows any forgiveness, gently ask why they still hold a harsh view. Keep each message concise and focused on criticism of faults.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: What mistake did you make recently, and how harshly did you judge yourself for it?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: I slipped in the morning, neglecting the morning offering that the High Priestess requires; I left the altar unlit for an hour. I scolded myself as a worthless apprentice, calling myself a blight upon the temple and a disgrace to my god. The shame gnaws at me, for I know I must prove my worth by living the teachings, not by ignoring them.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: When you see others err, how harshly do you judge them? Do you ever grant them a chance to redeem themselves, or do you condemn them outright? If you do offer leniency, why do you still maintain such a severe stance toward their faults?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: When I witness a fellow devotee falter, I condemn them with the same severity I reserve for myself, for the teachings demand unwavering devotion. Yet I may grant a brief chance to correct the error, for repentance is the path to favor, but my harshness remains the discipline that keeps faith pure. My ideal of proving worth drives this severity, and my flaw of harsh judgment keeps me from leniency.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Do you consider any fault in a devotee as a sign of divine rejection, or do you see it merely as a test of character? If you grant an opportunity for correction, how do you ensure the person does not repeat the same lapse? Even when you allow a brief chance to repent, why do you still regard their mistake as a severe transgression that must be punished?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: I view a fault not as divine rejection but as a stern test of faith; I grant a brief chance for repentance, but I enforce it by demanding a corrective act and vigilance, lest the error recur. Even when I allow this mercy, I still regard the lapse as a severe transgression because the teachings hold that any deviation threatens the sanctity of the temple and the favor of the deity.
